title: Possible Topological Decoherence Transition in Relativistic Electron Beams Propagating through Coulomb-Disordered Media
abstract: We show that the mutual coherence of a relativistic electron beam in a Coulomb-disordered medium is governed by an effective two-dimensional compact phase field with a logarithmic correlation function. The corresponding Gaussian free-field action exhibits a stiffness inversely proportional to the propagation length. When the compact nature of the phase is taken into account, the system supports vortex excitations that interact as a two-dimensional Coulomb gas. Renormalization-group analysis of this gas indicates the existence of a critical sample thickness $L_c$ at which a Berezinskii--Kosterlitz--Thouless (BKT) transition may occur, separating a regime of algebraic decoherence from one where free vortices proliferate and coherence is destroyed exponentially. The critical thickness is expressed through fundamental microscopic parameters and could be observed in transmission electron microscopy of liquid cells or cryogenic samples.
summary: This paper proposes that electron beams passing through disordered media undergo a topological transition similar to the BKT transition. As the sample thickness increases, the system moves from algebraic to exponential decoherence due to the proliferation of phase vortices. This effect is potentially observable in liquid-cell or cryo-electron microscopy.
main_problem: Investigating the asymptotic behavior of mutual coherence in relativistic electron beams passing through Coulomb-disordered media and determining if a BKT-type topological phase transition occurs as a function of sample thickness.
main_result: The study identifies a critical sample thickness Lc that separates a regime of algebraic decoherence from one of exponential decoherence driven by the proliferation of free vortices.
method: The author uses the eikonal approximation and a paraxial reduction of the Dirac equation, followed by a renormalization-group analysis of a 2D Coulomb gas model.
model_or_system: A relativistic electron beam propagating through a 3D classical one-component plasma (Coulomb-disordered medium) such as aqueous electrolytes or cryogenic samples, modeled via a 2D Gaussian free-field action.
key_observables: Mutual coherence function gamma(rho) and phase structure function Dphi(rho).
important_parameters: Critical thickness Lc, effective stiffness K, propagation length L, and the Debye screening length lambda_D.
main_assumptions: The phase is assumed to be compact (phi = phi + 2pi) to allow for vortices, and the potential is treated as a static Gaussian random potential.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of electron propagation through a fluctuating environment, the resulting logarithmic phase correlation, the mapping to a 2D Gaussian free field, and the introduction of vortex configurations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical problem of electron beam decoherence, derives an effective 2D field theory from the eikonal approximation, applies renormalization-group analysis to a vortex gas model, and concludes with experimental implications for TEM.
why_interesting: It demonstrates how a 3D disordered medium can give rise to an emergent 2D topological phase transition (BKT) in the coherence properties of a probe beam, bridging disordered systems and topological physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
